did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) LeNet and ResNet, to classify skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset. The study involved training these models on preprocessed skin lesion images and evaluating their performance using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score to distinguish between different types of skin conditions such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign keratosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation, training, and evaluation of LeNet and ResNet architectures on the HAM10000 dataset for skin lesion classification. It includes a proposed methodology, experimental results with performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score), and a conclusion based on these new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning for Dermatology: Analyzing Skin Lesions with LeNet and ResNet
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Electronics, Computing and Communication Technologies (CONECCT)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the performance of LeNet and ResNet convolutional neural networks for classifying skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers applied preprocessing techniques, including a Digital Hair Removal algorithm and median filtering, alongside data augmentation to address class imbalance. Both models were trained and evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The experimental results demonstrated that ResNet significantly outperformed the baseline LeNet architecture. Specifically, ResNet achieved an overall accuracy of 88%, compared to 73% for LeNet, highlighting the benefits of deeper architectures for complex skin lesion classification.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (melanocytic nevi, melanoma, benign keratosis-like lesions, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, vascular lesions, and dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements LeNet and ResNet architectures to classify skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset into seven specific diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with confusion matrices provided for each class. The task is explicitly defined as multi-class classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of skin lesion images used for classification. The paper notes it is large but suffers from class imbalance.

use.training: 800 images were used for training the LeNet and ResNet models.
use.validation: The paper mentions calculating loss and accuracy on validation sets and plotting curves, but does not specify the number of images or split ratio for validation.
use.testing: The remaining images (200) were used for testing.
use.other: Data augmentation was applied to the training set to balance classes and prevent bias.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparison between LeNet and ResNet was based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.experiment: Comparison of LeNet and ResNet architectures with preprocessing (Digital Hair Removal, Median Filter) and Data Augmentation
HAM10000.notes: ResNet significantly outperformed LeNet in overall accuracy (88% vs 73%). The paper notes that training and validation losses were reducing for both architectures, indicating no overfitting. Confusion matrices were generated for both models.

architecture_details.LeNet: Shallow CNN, baseline model, computationally efficient, fewer layers.
architecture_details.ResNet: Deep residual learning framework, deep architecture, uses more layers for feature extraction, designed to tackle vanishing gradient problems.

preprocessing_techniques: Digital Hair Removal (DHR) algorithm involving grayscale conversion, black hat filtering, mask creation for inpainting, and hair removal

Data augmentation to balance classes and prevent bias, including: rotating image, adding Gaussian noise, cropping image, hue (change color), elastic transformation (water-like effect), and coarse dropout

conclusions: ResNet significantly outperformed the baseline LeNet architecture in classifying skin lesions, achieving an overall accuracy of 88% compared to LeNet's 73%.

The superior performance of ResNet is attributed to its deeper architecture with more layers for feature extraction and its residual learning framework, which helps tackle vanishing gradient problems.
LeNet, while computationally efficient due to fewer layers, demonstrated lower performance metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) across most lesion classes compared to ResNet.
The application of preprocessing techniques (Digital Hair Removal, median filtering) and data augmentation helped address class imbalance and noise, contributing to the models' effectiveness.
Both LeNet and ResNet architectures showed reducing training and validation losses, indicating that the models did not overfit the training data.

Future work should focus on using deeper neural networks or increasing network size to further improve classification performance and expanding the dataset to include patient demographic details (age, sex, skin color) for more comprehensive analysis.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small subset of the HAM10000 dataset (1,000 images total: 800 training, 200 testing), which limits the generalizability of the results compared to studies using the full dataset.

The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it impossible to assess the statistical significance of the difference between LeNet (73%) and ResNet (88%).
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal test split of the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about external validity.

The paper claims to use 'transfer learning' in the conclusion to explain ResNet's performance, but the methodology section does not specify if pre-trained weights were used, creating a contradiction or ambiguity in the experimental setup.

The dataset suffers from inherent class imbalance in HAM10000, which the authors attempted to mitigate via data augmentation, but the specific augmentation ratios or techniques per class are not detailed quantitatively.
The paper does not report hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, batch size, optimizer, number of epochs), limiting the reproducibility of the study.

The preprocessing pipeline includes a custom Digital Hair Removal (DHR) algorithm and median filtering, but the impact of these specific preprocessing steps on the final performance is not ablated or isolated.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope, focusing only on comparing two architectures without exploring more advanced state-of-the-art models or clinical deployment challenges.
